blocks.body: Relative Clauses shows you how to add information about people, things, places, and possession without starting a new sentence.

{"level":"B1-B2","intro":"Relative clauses let you add description to a noun without creating a completely new sentence. They help your English sound more precise and natural.","rules":[{"label":"People / things / places","color":"emerald","text":"We most often use who, which, that, whose, and where.","example":"The girl who sits next to me is from Lviv."},{"label":"One sentence, more detail","color":"blue","text":"A relative clause attaches to a noun and adds information about it inside the same sentence.","example":"This is the book that I told you about."},{"label":"Subject or object","color":"amber","text":"It is important to see whether the relative pronoun works as the subject or the object, because that changes the form.","example":"The man who called me / The man who I called"}]}

blocks.heading: 1. What a relative clause is
blocks.body:
A relative clause is a part of the sentence that describes the noun before it.

  • The teacher who helped us was very patient.
  • I like films that have a strong ending.
  • The cafe where we met is still open.

Instead of two short sentences, you get one connected structure.

blocks.heading: 2. The main words
blocks.body:
WordTypical useExample
whopeopleThe woman who works here is my cousin.
whichthings / animalsThe phone which I bought is already broken.
thatpeople or things in many defining clausesThe song that you sent me was great.
whosepossessionI know a boy whose father is a pilot.
whereplaceThis is the town where my parents met.

blocks.heading: 3. Subject clause vs object clause
blocks.body:
Compare these two patterns:

  • Subject relative clause: The woman who lives next door is a doctor.
    Here who is the subject inside the relative clause.
  • Object relative clause: The woman who I met yesterday is a doctor.
    Here who is the object, and I is the subject.

This matters because in an object clause the relative pronoun can sometimes disappear: The woman I met yesterday...

blocks.heading: 4. Common mistakes
blocks.body:
• Mistake: mixing up who and which. For people, who is usually the right choice.
  • Mistake: forgetting the verb in the relative clause. The boy who very friendly is incomplete.
  • Mistake: adding an extra pronoun: The man who he called. After who, the extra he is not needed.

blocks.heading: 5. Quick rule
blocks.body:
• Relative clauses describe a noun inside the same sentence.
  • who = people, which = things, whose = possession, where = place.
  • that is common in defining clauses.
  • Object relative clauses matter because that is where the pronoun can often be omitted.
